analogy: Think of "Social Movements" through the chapter's own opening images: A great many students and office-workers around the world go to work only for five or six days and rest on the weekends. Yet, very few people who relax on their day off realise that this holiday is the outcome of a long struggle by... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 8.1 Features oF a Social Movement, 8.6 Caste B ased MoveMents, Social Movements, Social. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Social Movements is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that a great many students and office-workers around the world go to work only for five or six days and rest on the weekends.

The text explains that yet, very few people who relax on their day off realise that this holiday is the outcome of a long struggle by workers.

The text explains that that the work-day should not exceed eight hours, that men and women should be paid equally for doing the same work, that workers are entitled to social security and pension — these and many other rights were gained through social movements.

The text explains that social movements have shaped the world we live in and continue to do so.

The text explains that we often assume that the rights we enjoy just happened to exist.

The text explains that it is important to recall the struggles of the past, which made these rights possible.

The text explains that you have read about the 19th century social reform movements, of the struggles against caste and gender discrimination and of the nationalist movement in India that brought us independence from colonial rule in 1947.

The text explains that you are familiar also with the many nationalist movements around the world in Asia, Africa and Americas that put an end to colonial rule.

The text explains that the socialist movements world over, the civil rights movement in the United States in the 1950s and 1960s that fought for equal rights for Blacks, the anti-apartheid struggle in South Africa have all changed the world in fundamental ways.

The text explains that social movements not The Right to Vote Universal adult franchise, or the right of every adult to vote, is one of the foremost rights guaranteed by the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that it means that we cannot be governed by anyone other than the people we have ourselves elected to represent us.

The text explains that this right is a radical departure from the days of colonial rule when ordinary people were forced to submit to the authority of colonial officers who represented the interests of the British Crown.

The text explains that however, even in Britain, not everyone was allowed to vote.

The text explains that voting rights were limited to property-owning men.

The text explains that chartism was a social movement for parliamentary representation in England.

The text explains that in 1839, more than 1.25 million people signed the People’s Charter asking for universal male suffrage, voting by ballot, and the right to stand for elections without owning property.

The text explains that yet, it was only after World War I, in 1918 that all men over 21, married women, women owning houses, and women university graduates over the age of 30, got the right to vote.

The text explains that when the suffragettes (women activists) took up the cause of all adult women’s right to vote, they were bitterly opposed and their movement violently crushed.

The text explains that what are the rights you take for granted in your life and which she did not have?

The text explains that you saw in Chapter 3 how the I n d i a n n a t i o n a l m o v e m e n t shaped the making of the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that and how in turn the Indian Constitution played a major role in bringing about social change.

The text explains that 8.1 Features oF a Social Movement A social movement requires sustained collective action over time.

The text explains that such action is often directed against the state and takes the form of demanding changes in state policy or practice.

The text explains that this organisation may include a leadership and a structure that defines how members relate to each other, make decisions and carry them out.

The text explains that social movements often arise with the aim of bringing about changes on a public issue, such as ensuring the right of the tribal population to use the forests or the right of displaced people to settlement and compensation.

The text explains that when reformers demanded education for girls, many protested that this would be disastrous for society.

The text explains that social movements cannot change society easily.

The text explains that social movement Try and think of any example that will show you how society is changed by social movements and also how a social movement can lead to other social movements.

The text explains that social movements also chart out campaigns that include lobbying with the government, media and other important makers of public opinion.

The text explains that you will recall this discussion from Chapter 3.

The text explains that this could be candle and torch light processions, use of black cloth, street theatres, songs, poetry.

The text explains that mahatma Gandhi wore khadi, hand-spun, hand-woven cloth, to support Indian cotton-growers, spinners and weavers whose livelihoods had been destroyed by the government policy of favouring mill-made cloth.

The text explains that the broad historical processes of social change are the sum total of countless individual and collective actions gathered across time and space.

The text explains that social movements are directed towards some specific goals.

The text explains that to draw from our discussion in Chapter 2, we can view sanskritisation and westernisation as social changes and see the 19th century social reformers’ efforts to change the society as social movements.

The text explains that food riots in England were often suppressed by the government.

The text explains that these protests were perceived by elites as a major threat to the established order of society.

The text explains that durkheim’s writings about the division of labour in society, forms of religious life, and even suicide, mirror his concern about how social structures enable social integration.

The text explains that thompson showed that the ‘crowd’ and the ‘mob’ were not made up of anarchic hooligans out to destroy society.

The text explains that in other words, they have their own shared understanding of right and wrong that informed their actions.

The text explains that their research showed that poor people in urban areas had good reasons for protesting.

The text explains that the 1960s movement for the reorganisation of Indian states on the basis of language and the recent Right to Information campaign are examples of reformist movements.

The text explains that so the ‘new’ social movements were not about changing the distribution of power in society but about quality-of-life issues such as having a clean environment.

The text explains that people left out by the formal political system join social movements or non-party political formations in order to put pressure on the state from outside.

The text explains that 2004. ‘The Working Class Movement in India : Trade Unions and the State’ in Manoranjan Mohanti Class, Caste and Gender.

The text explains that the Indian Women’s Movement: Reform and Revival.

The text explains that in Yogendra Singh (Ed.), Indian Sociology: Emerging Concepts, Structure and Change.

The text explains that veena Das, The Oxford India Companion to Sociology and Social Anthropology. pp.

The text explains that 2002. “Chipko: Social History of an Environmental Movement” in Shah Ghansyam Social Movements and the State.

The text explains that ethnicity and Gender: Select Essays on Tribes.

The text explains that kinship Terminology and Marriage Rules: The Khasis of North- East India.

The text explains that nation, Civil Society and Social Movements: Essays in Political Sociology.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Social Change and Development in India, Chapter 8: Social Movements

prelims_traps: Do not treat 8.1 Features oF a Social Movement and 8.6 Caste B ased MoveMents as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Social Movements; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Social Movements through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 8.1 Features oF a Social Movement and 8.6 Caste B ased MoveMents to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
